title: Intel 8085 I/O Read Timing Diagram (IN Instruction)
examTip: The I/O Read cycle is similar to the Memory Read cycle, but IO/M̄ is HIGH (1) instead of LOW. It consists of 3 T-states (T1-T3). In T1, the 8-bit I/O port address is sent on BOTH A8-A15 AND AD0-AD7 (duplicated), and ALE goes high. In T2, RD̄ goes low. In T3, the CPU reads data from the peripheral on the AD0-AD7 bus. Status: S1=1, S0=1 (IO Read). IO/M̄ = 1 throughout.

blocks.label: I/O Port Address (Duplicated)
blocks.note: Same 8-bit address on A8-A15 AND AD0-AD7

blocks.label: IO/M̄ = 1 (HIGH — I/O operation, not memory)
blocks.note: HIGH distinguishes I/O from Memory Read

blocks.label: S1 = 1, S0 = 1 (I/O Read Status)
blocks.note: S1=1 S0=1 uniquely identifies I/O Read cycle

blocks.label: Key Difference from Memory Read: IO/M̄ = 1 (HIGH) vs IO/M̄ = 0 (LOW). Port address 8-bit duplicated on A8-A15 and AD0-AD7. Status: S1=1, S0=1.

theory:
The I/O Read machine cycle (executed by the 8085's IN instruction) reads one byte of data from a specified 8-bit I/O port address. It is structurally similar to the Memory Read cycle, but differs in two critical ways: (1) IO/M̄ is HIGH instead of LOW, and (2) the 8-bit port address is duplicated on both the upper address bus (A8-A15) and the lower multiplexed bus (AD0-AD7).

Step-by-Step Operation:

1. State T1:
- The 8-bit I/O port address (from the IN instruction operand) is placed simultaneously on both A8-A15 (upper address bus) and AD0-AD7 (lower multiplexed bus). This duplication means the full 16-bit bus carries the same 8-bit address on both halves.
- IO/M̄ is set HIGH to distinguish this as an I/O cycle (not a memory cycle).
- Status bits S1=1, S0=1 identify this as an I/O Read operation.
- ALE (Address Latch Enable) pulses HIGH. The falling edge of ALE causes an external latch (e.g., 74LS373) to capture the 8-bit port address from AD0-AD7.

2. State T2:
- The multiplexed AD0-AD7 bus releases the port address and briefly floats (Hi-Z turnaround period).
- The CPU asserts RD̄ LOW, enabling the output buffers of the addressed I/O port device.
- The peripheral device places its 8-bit data onto the AD0-AD7 bus.

3. State T3:
- Data from the peripheral remains stable on AD0-AD7.
- The CPU samples and latches this data into the Accumulator on the rising clock edge.
- RD̄ returns HIGH, disabling the peripheral's output buffers. The bus returns to Hi-Z.
keyPoints: The 8085 I/O Read cycle (IN instruction) consists of exactly 3 T-states: T1, T2, T3.
IO/M̄ = 1 (HIGH) throughout — this is the key difference from Memory Read (IO/M̄ = 0).
The 8-bit I/O port address is duplicated: placed on both A8-A15 AND AD0-AD7 simultaneously in T1.
ALE pulses HIGH in T1, latching the port address into an external decoder/latch.
RD̄ goes LOW in T2 (activates peripheral output); data is read by CPU in T3; RD̄ goes HIGH again.
Status bits S1=1, S0=1 uniquely identify the I/O Read machine cycle.
useCases: Analyzing bus timing when reading data from a peripheral IC connected to the 8085.
Understanding the execution of the IN instruction in 8085 assembly language.
Distinguishing I/O and memory operations using IO/M̄ and status signals.
